content:
Incident Report
Page 9 of 11
was one of the involved shooting officers. Deputy Oleole was wearing a white and blue hat, sunglasses, grey t-shirt, blue pants and black shoes. On top of his shirt, he was wearing a dark blue ballistic vest with miscellaneous equipment attached to the vest. He was wearing a belt, which his holster was attached to. In his left front pants pocket, he was carrying a pocket knife, his keys and chapstick. In his right front pants pocket he was carrying a can of tobacco. I took photos of his keys, chapstick, and tobacco and let him take them with him. I asked Deputy Oleole if he had all of the equipment he had on at the time of the incident. He told me it was not and said his rifle, which was the only weapon he fired during the incident, was still in the vehicle at the crime scene . He said the rifle was in a silver Ford Escape. He said the silver Ford Escape was the vehicle that was nose to nose with the suspect vehicle.
I took overall photos of Deputy Oleole bearing all of his equipment. I then took close up photos of his equipment. After getting photos of Deputy Oleole's gear, I had him remove his handgun from his holster, which he then gave to me. Deputy Oleole's handgun is a Glock 17 9mm (Serial Number: BBRC916) . I removed the magazine and cleared the firearm. As I was clearing the firearm, I observed a round extract from the chamber. The magazine that I removed from the handgun was loaded with 21 rounds. I then counted Deputy Oleole's backup handgun magazines and each were loaded with 21 rounds. I counted 31 rounds in Deputy Oleole's rifle magazine, which was kept in his right chest pocket. I then asked Deputy Oleole to remove all of his gear. Deputy Oleole had a change of clothes brought to him. I asked Deputy Oleole to change into his clothes as we would be taking his gear and clothing as evidence.
As I was processing Deputy Oleole's gear, Detective Goss was annotating everything onto a property in custody form, placing the items into paper bags and labeling the paper bags accordingly with Oleole's name, the item number reflected on the property in custody sheet, and the item nomenclature.
After processing Deputy Oleole, Detective Goss and I began to process the remaining witness officers. We were instructed not to take any gear from the witness officers. We were instructed to only take overall photos and do a round count of all the ammunition on their person.
Detective Goss and I were then introduced to Pierce County Deputy John F. Munson. I began by taking overall photos of Deputy Munson. I documented Deputy Munson's Glock 17 9mm pistol (serial number: BDAT231) . The magazine that was inserted into the handgun contained 21 rounds. As I cleared the handgun, I observed one round extract from the chamber. Deputy Munson had two backup handgun magazines and one rifle magazine. Both backup handgun magazines contained 21, 9mm rounds each. His rifle magazine contained 29, .223 rounds.
We were then introduced to Pierce County Detective Sergeant Byron M. Brockway. I began by taking overall photos of Detective Sergeant Brockway. I documented Detective Sergeant Brockway's Glock 17 9mm pistol (serial number: BGMB608) . The magazine that was inserted into the handgun contained 16 rounds. As I cleared the handgun, I observed one round extract from the chamber. Detective Sergeant Brockway's had two backup handgun magazines and one rifle magazine. Both backup handgun magazines contained 17, 9mm rounds each. His rifle magazine contained
